title: Quiet Quitting as Anger Without Words
dek: You call it setting boundaries. Your body calls it a slow freeze. When we cannot speak our fury, we swallow it into the fascia, letting the jaw tighten and the psoas grip while we pretend we are simply disengaging from the grind.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Slow Freeze of the Unspoken

You sit at your desk, the blue light of the monitor washing over your face, and you tell yourself you are simply setting boundaries. You are "quiet quitting." You are doing exactly what you are paid to do, no more, no less. It sounds so reasonable, so measured, so incredibly mature. But beneath the surface of that calm detachment, your body is telling a different story. The masseter muscle in your jaw is clenched so tight it hums. Your shoulders have crept up toward your ears, the trapezius locked in a permanent shrug of defense. Your breath is shallow, barely moving the diaphragm, trapped somewhere high in your chest. 

This is not peace. This is a slow freeze. This is anger without words.

We are taught, especially as women, that fury is ugly. We are taught that it is unprofessional, unspiritual, and ultimately destructive. So when the demands of the world become too much, when the disrespect is palpable but deniable, we do not scream. We do not flip the desk. We withdraw. We quiet quit. But the body knows. The body always knows. The anger does not evaporate just because we refuse to give it a voice. It simply moves inward, settling into the fascia, the muscles, the very architecture of our being.

## The Biology of Disengagement

When you quiet quit, you are not just changing your relationship to your job; you are changing your relationship to your own vitality. Gabor Maté, in his unflinching exploration of the mind-body connection, reminds us that when we suppress our authentic emotions, the body bears the burden. He writes about how the repression of anger is not merely a psychological defense mechanism but a physiological event. The immune system, the nervous system, the endocrine system—they all respond to the unspoken "no." 

Your quiet quitting is a "no" that you have swallowed. It is a refusal to participate in a system that drains you, but because it is unexpressed, it turns into a low-grade, chronic stress response. The cortisol still pumps. The adrenaline still spikes. But there is no release. There is no action. You are a car with the accelerator pressed to the floor and the parking brake engaged. The engine is screaming, but you are going nowhere.

Consider the physical sensation of this disengagement. Notice the grip in your psoas, the deep muscle that connects your spine to your legs, the muscle that prepares you to run or fight. It is tight, isn't it? It is waiting for a signal that never comes. You are holding yourself back, literally. 

## The Medicine of Acknowledgment

We must begin to treat this not as a moral failure or a lack of ambition, but as a biological reality. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body is doing exactly what it was designed to do: it is protecting you from a threat. But the protection has become a prison.

To begin thawing the freeze, we must first acknowledge the ice. We must name the anger. You do not have to shout it from the rooftops. You do not have to send a blistering email to your boss. But you must tell the truth to your own bones. 

I often recommend [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) to women who are in this state of chronic, suppressed activation. It is an adaptogen, a serious medicine that helps the nervous system remember how to regulate itself. It does not numb the anger; it gives the body the resilience to hold it without shattering. It supports the adrenal glands, which have been working overtime to manage the unspoken stress of your quiet rebellion.

## The Somatic Reality of Fury

Let us look at the anatomy of your silence. When you receive that passive-aggressive email at 4:00 PM on a Friday, notice what happens. You do not reply. You close the laptop. You tell yourself you are disengaging. But feel the back of your neck. The levator scapulae, the muscle that lifts your shoulder blade, is rigid. Your stomach, the seat of your enteric nervous system, is tied in a knot. 

Bessel van der Kolk has taught us that the body keeps the score. The trauma of chronic invalidation, of being treated as a cog in a machine, leaves a physical imprint. The anger you feel is a valid, necessary response to an environment that does not honor your humanity. When you quiet quit, you are attempting to protect your humanity, but you are doing so by cutting off a vital part of your emotional spectrum.

You cannot selectively numb your emotions. When you numb the anger, you numb the joy. When you freeze the fury, you freeze the passion. The quiet quitting bleeds into your life outside of work. You become quiet in your relationships. You become quiet in your desires. The slow freeze takes over.

## Thawing the Ice: A Practice

We must invite the anger back into the room, not as a destructive force, but as a messenger. We must listen to what the body is trying to say.

Here is a short embodied practice to begin the thaw:

Find a quiet place where you will not be disturbed. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, feeling the solid ground beneath you. Close your eyes. Bring your attention to your jaw. Notice the tension there. Do not try to force it to relax. Just notice it. Now, take a deep breath in through your nose, and as you exhale, let your jaw drop open slightly. Make a soft, low sound—a sigh, a groan, a hum. Let the sound vibrate in your throat and your chest. 

Bring your attention to your hands. Are they clenched? Let them open. Shake them out, vigorously, as if you are shaking off water. Let the movement travel up your arms to your shoulders. Shake your shoulders. Let the trapped energy begin to move. 

If you feel the urge to make a louder sound, do it. If you feel the urge to stomp your feet, do it. Let the body express the "no" that you have been swallowing. This is not about losing control; it is about regaining it. It is about letting the energy of the anger move through you, rather than getting stuck in your tissues.

After this practice, you might find that your body craves deep rest. The release of that held energy can be exhausting. This is when I suggest a warm bath with [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20). The magnesium in the salts helps to soothe the muscles that have been gripping so tightly. It is a way of telling your body, "You are safe now. You can let go."

## The Courage to Speak

Quiet quitting is a survival strategy, and there is no shame in surviving. But it is not a long-term solution for a vibrant life. Eventually, the anger must find a voice. It must be translated from the language of the fascia into the language of the world.

This does not mean you have to become aggressive. It means you have to become authentic. It means recognizing that your anger is a sign that your boundaries have been crossed, that your values have been compromised. It is a fierce, protective energy that can be harnessed for change.

When we begin to speak our truth, even in small ways, the body responds. The breath deepens. The shoulders drop. The jaw softens. We reclaim our vitality. We step out of the freeze and back into the flow of life.

Sometimes, the physical tension is so deeply ingrained that we need external support to release it. A [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a powerful tool for this. Lying on the mat stimulates blood flow and triggers the release of endorphins, helping to break up the physical stagnation that accompanies suppressed anger. It is a somatic intervention for a somatic problem.

## The Alchemy of Anger

Anger is not the enemy. The suppression of anger is the enemy. When we learn to listen to our fury, to feel it in our bodies without being consumed by it, it becomes a powerful catalyst for transformation. It tells us what needs to change. It gives us the energy to make those changes.

Susan Drury knows this. The herbs know this. The body knows this. We are not meant to be quiet, compliant machines. We are meant to be messy, vibrant, feeling creatures. We are meant to experience the full spectrum of our humanity, including the fierce, protective fire of our anger.

So the next time you find yourself quiet quitting, the next time you feel that slow freeze creeping in, pause. Notice your body. Notice the clenched jaw, the tight psoas, the shallow breath. Acknowledge the anger. Welcome it as a messenger. And then, slowly, carefully, begin to give it a voice.

## FAQ

**Q:** Is quiet quitting always a sign of suppressed anger?
**A:** Not always, but very often. Sometimes it is a conscious, healthy recalibration of boundaries. But if it is accompanied by physical tension, resentment, or a sense of deadness, it is likely that anger is operating beneath the surface. The body's response is the key indicator.

**Q:** How can I express my anger professionally?
**A:** Expressing anger does not mean yelling or being abusive. It means clearly and firmly stating your boundaries and needs. It means saying, "I cannot take on this additional project without adjusting my current workload," rather than silently seething and doing the bare minimum. It is about clarity, not volume.

**Q:** What if my workplace is toxic and speaking up is dangerous?
**A:** This is a very real concern. If speaking up will jeopardize your livelihood or safety, quiet quitting may be the safest option for now. But you must still find ways to process the anger outside of work—through somatic practices, physical activity, or creative expression—so it does not become trapped in your body.

**Q:** Can herbs really help with anger?
**A:** Herbs cannot change a toxic boss or an unfair system. But they can support your nervous system in handling the stress of those situations. Adaptogens like Ashwagandha help regulate cortisol, while nervines like Chamomile can soothe a frayed nervous system. They are allies in your somatic healing, not magic cures.

## The Deep Roots of the Freeze

Let us delve deeper into the anatomy of this silence. When you are sitting in that meeting, the one that could have been an email, and someone takes credit for your work, what happens in your body? You do not speak up. You smile a tight, professional smile. But beneath the desk, your toes are curled. Your calves are tight. The energy of the anger, the biological imperative to defend your territory, has been activated, but it has nowhere to go. It pools in your lower extremities, creating a sense of heaviness, of being rooted to the spot in the worst possible way.

This is the legacy of a culture that demands compliance over authenticity. We have been trained, from a very young age, to prioritize the comfort of others over our own truth. We have been taught that to be a "good girl," a "team player," a "professional," we must swallow our objections. We must smooth over the rough edges. We must never, ever make a scene.

But the body does not understand "professionalism." The body understands threat and safety. When your work is stolen, when your boundaries are ignored, the body registers a threat. The sympathetic nervous system kicks into gear. The heart rate increases. The pupils dilate. The blood flows away from the digestive organs and toward the muscles, preparing for action. 

And then... nothing. You force yourself to remain still. You force yourself to remain quiet. You override the biological imperative with a cognitive command. This override takes an immense amount of energy. It is exhausting. This is why you feel so drained at the end of a day where you have seemingly done nothing but sit at a desk. You have been fighting a war inside your own body.

## The Cost of Compliance

The cost of this constant suppression is high. It is not just the physical tension, the headaches, the digestive issues. It is the slow erosion of your sense of self. When you repeatedly ignore your own anger, you send a message to yourself that your feelings do not matter. You begin to distrust your own instincts. You become disconnected from your own internal compass.

This disconnection is the true tragedy of quiet quitting. It is not that you are doing less work for your employer; it is that you are doing less living for yourself. You are shrinking your world to avoid the conflict, but in doing so, you are also shrinking your capacity for joy, for passion, for connection.

We must recognize that anger is a vital life force. It is the energy of boundary-setting. It is the energy of self-protection. It is the energy that says, "I matter. My needs matter. My boundaries matter." When we cut ourselves off from this energy, we cut ourselves off from our own power.

## Reclaiming the Fire

How do we begin to reclaim this fire? How do we thaw the freeze and invite the anger back into our lives in a healthy, constructive way?

It begins with the body. It begins with noticing the physical sensations of anger and allowing them to be there without judgment. When you feel that tightness in your chest, that heat in your face, do not try to push it away. Do not try to rationalize it or explain it away. Just feel it. Breathe into it. Let it be what it is: a biological response to a perceived threat.

This is where somatic practices become essential. We must learn to speak the language of the body. We must learn to move the energy of the anger through our tissues, rather than letting it stagnate. This might mean vigorous exercise, like running or kickboxing. It might mean expressive movement, like dancing or shaking. It might mean vocalization, like singing or shouting in a safe space.

The goal is not to get rid of the anger, but to process it. To let it complete its biological cycle. When the energy has moved through you, you will find that the physical tension begins to release. The jaw softens. The shoulders drop. The breath deepens. You return to a state of regulation.

## The Role of the Nervous System

Understanding the nervous system is crucial in this process. When we are in a state of chronic suppression, our nervous system becomes dysregulated. We may become stuck in a state of sympathetic arousal (fight or flight) or dorsal vagal shutdown (freeze). Quiet quitting is often a manifestation of this freeze state. It is a profound disengagement from the environment, a way of playing dead to survive.

To move out of the freeze state, we must gently stimulate the nervous system, bringing it back online. This requires safety and connection. It requires finding spaces and people where we feel seen, heard, and validated. It requires practices that ground us in the present moment and remind us that we are safe.

This is where the herbs can be profound allies. We have already spoken of Ashwagandha, but there are others. Milky Oats, for example, is a deeply nourishing tonic for a frayed nervous system. It is like food for the nerves, helping to rebuild resilience after periods of chronic stress. Skullcap is another powerful ally, helping to soothe the agitation and anxiety that often accompany suppressed anger. These are not quick fixes; they are slow, deep medicines that work over time to restore balance to the body.

## The Intersection of Somatics and Society

We cannot talk about quiet quitting and suppressed anger without talking about the societal structures that create these conditions. Resmaa Menakem, in his groundbreaking work on somatic abolitionism, reminds us that trauma and oppression are not just psychological experiences; they are held in the body. The chronic stress of navigating a society that devalues your labor, your identity, or your humanity leaves a physical imprint.

For many women, particularly women of color, the workplace is a site of constant microaggressions and systemic invalidation. The anger that arises in response to this is not only justified; it is a necessary survival mechanism. But the expression of that anger is heavily policed. The "angry Black woman" trope, for example, is a powerful tool used to silence and dismiss legitimate grievances.

In this context, quiet quitting can be seen as a form of somatic resistance. It is a refusal to give more of your life force to a system that does not value you. It is a way of preserving your energy for yourself, your family, your community. But we must be careful that this resistance does not turn into self-harm. We must ensure that the anger is still being processed, still being felt, even if it is not being expressed in the workplace.

## The Practice of Presence

The antidote to the slow freeze is presence. It is the willingness to be fully in your body, feeling whatever is there to be felt. This is not easy. It requires courage. It requires a willingness to be uncomfortable. But it is the only way to truly live.

When you are present with your anger, you are present with your power. You are no longer a victim of your circumstances; you are an active participant in your own life. You can choose how to respond, rather than reacting from a place of conditioning or fear.

This presence is a practice. It is something we must cultivate every day. It is in the way we breathe, the way we walk, the way we interact with others. It is in the small moments of choosing authenticity over compliance, of choosing truth over comfort.

## The Wisdom of the Body

Your body is not a machine. It is a living, breathing ecosystem, deeply connected to the world around it. It has its own wisdom, its own intelligence. When we learn to listen to this wisdom, we find that it is always guiding us toward healing, toward wholeness.

The anger is a part of this wisdom. It is a fierce, protective guardian, alerting us when our boundaries have been crossed, when our values have been compromised. When we honor this guardian, when we give it a voice, we reclaim our vitality. We step out of the freeze and back into the flow of life.

So let the jaw unclench. Let the shoulders drop. Let the breath deepen. Feel the fire in your belly, the strength in your legs. You are alive. You are powerful. And your anger is a sacred messenger, guiding you home to yourself.

## The Journey Forward

As we navigate the complexities of modern work and life, let us remember that we are not alone. We are part of a long lineage of women who have had to navigate the tension between compliance and authenticity. We can draw strength from their stories, from their resilience, from their fierce, unspoken anger.

We can also draw strength from the natural world. The herbs, the trees, the earth itself—they are all allies in our healing. They remind us of the cycles of life, of the necessity of both the storm and the calm. They remind us that we are part of something larger than ourselves, something ancient and wise.

The journey of thawing the freeze is not a linear one. There will be days when the anger feels overwhelming, and days when the numbness returns. But with practice, with patience, with a deep commitment to our own somatic truth, we can find our way back to ourselves. We can learn to speak our fury, not as a destructive force, but as a powerful catalyst for change. And in doing so, we can create a life that is truly our own.
